title: Floating Point Numbers
summary: Floating point represents real numbers in computers using a sign, exponent, and mantissa. IEEE 754 is the universal standard for 32-bit and 64-bit floating point.
content_html:
Representing Real Numbers

Integers are easy. Real numbers are hard — they can be infinitely precise (π = 3.14159265358979…). Computers must approximate them using finite bits.

Fixed-Point Representation

Dedicate a fixed number of bits to the integer part and the fractional part. The binary point is at a fixed location.

01010.101₂ = 2²+2⁰ + 2⁻¹+2⁻³ = 4+1 + 0.5+0.125 = 5.625

Limitation: The range and precision are fixed — can be wasteful for very large or very small numbers.

Floating Point — Scientific Notation Approach

Floating point trades off range vs precision dynamically, like scientific notation:

3.14159 × 10²  =  Sign(+) × Mantissa(3.14159) × Base(10)^Exponent(2)

IEEE 754: 32-bit Single Precision

The universal standard for floating point arithmetic. A 32-bit float has 3 parts:

BitsFieldPurpose
1 bitSign0 = positive, 1 = negative
8 bitsExponentBiased by 127 (actual exponent = stored - 127)
23 bitsMantissa (fraction)Fractional part of the normalised significand
Value = (-1)^sign × 1.mantissa × 2^(exponent-127)
Normalisation: The leading 1 before the binary point is implied — it's not stored. This gives an extra bit of precision for free.

IEEE 754: 64-bit Double Precision

Field32-bit (float)64-bit (double)
Sign1 bit1 bit
Exponent8 bits (bias 127)11 bits (bias 1023)
Mantissa23 bits52 bits
Total32 bits64 bits

Special Values in IEEE 754

ExponentMantissaMeaning
0 (all 0s)0Zero (±0)
0 (all 0s)non-zeroDenormalised number (very small)
255 (all 1s)0Infinity (±∞)
255 (all 1s)non-zeroNaN (Not a Number)

Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD)

Each decimal digit stored as a 4-bit nibble. Example: 7926.34 → 0111 1001 0010 0110 . 0011 0100. Used in simple calculators and financial software where exact decimal representation is needed.

Precision warning: Not all real numbers can be represented exactly in binary floating point. For example, 0.1 in decimal has no exact binary representation, which can cause rounding errors.

exercises.title: IEEE 754 sign bit
exercises.prompt: In IEEE 754 single precision, how many bits are used for the sign?

exercises.title: Exponent bits
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in the exponent field of a 32-bit IEEE 754 float?

exercises.title: Mantissa bits
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in the mantissa (fraction) of a 32-bit IEEE 754 float?

exercises.title: Exponent bias
exercises.prompt: The exponent bias for 32-bit IEEE 754 is:

exercises.title: NaN
exercises.prompt: In IEEE 754, NaN (Not a Number) is represented by:
exercises.options_json: ["All exponent bits 0, mantissa non-zero", "All exponent bits 1, mantissa zero", "All exponent bits 1, mantissa non-zero", "All bits zero"]

exercises.title: Double precision mantissa
exercises.prompt: A 64-bit IEEE 754 double has how many mantissa bits?

exercises.title: Implied leading 1
exercises.prompt: Why is the leading 1 of the mantissa not stored in IEEE 754?
exercises.options_json: ["It saves 1 bit of precision since it's always 1 for normalised numbers", "It makes arithmetic simpler", "It avoids negative numbers", "Standards committee decided so"]
exercises.answer: It saves 1 bit of precision since it's always 1 for normalised numbers

exercises.title: Fixed-point limitation
exercises.prompt: The main limitation of fixed-point representation is:
exercises.options_json: ["Cannot represent fractions", "Fixed precision wastes bits for numbers needing different precision", "Cannot represent integers", "Requires two's complement"]
exercises.answer: Fixed precision wastes bits for numbers needing different precision

exercises.title: BCD usage
exercises.prompt: Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD) is commonly used in:
exercises.options_json: ["GPU shader programs", "Financial and calculator applications needing exact decimal representation", "ARM assembly code", "Operating system kernels"]
exercises.answer: Financial and calculator applications needing exact decimal representation

exercises.title: Infinity representation
exercises.prompt: Positive infinity in IEEE 754 is represented by:
exercises.options_json: ["All exponent bits 1 and mantissa 0", "All bits 1", "All exponent bits 0 and mantissa 1", "Sign=0, exponent=0, mantissa=0"]
